title: Ginger nuts
subtitle: Hard-snap ginger biscuits with black treacle
excerpt: Ginger nuts are a hard-snap biscuit, not a soft one. They are rolled into balls from a stiff dough and flatten during baking; there is no rolling or cutting. The black treacle and ginger combination gives them their distinctive dark colour and sharp heat.
sourceNotes: Ginger nuts are a British biscuit with roots in medieval gingerbread. The hard-snap baked variety became standardised in British domestic cookery through the Victorian period. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — ginger biscuits appear in the biscuit chapter with the treacle and ground ginger as the defining flavourings.

recipe.batchNotes: These keep for 3–4 weeks in an airtight tin — make a large batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 4 weeks. They harden further after the first day.

glossaryTerms.definition: The hard, brittle texture of a properly made ginger nut. It is produced by the combination of melted fat (not creamed butter), golden syrup and treacle, and a high bake temperature. Ginger nuts spread flat during baking and the moisture drives off completely, leaving behind a dense, dry biscuit that cracks when broken rather than bending. They should feel completely rigid when tapped.

content.text: comes from a specific method: the butter, syrup, and treacle are melted together before being added to the dry ingredients, and the biscuits are baked at a high temperature until completely dry. They will feel slightly soft in the centre when they come out of the oven but harden entirely as they cool. Do not be tempted to extend the bake time.

Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{black-treacle}} together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat. Combine {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{salt-table}} in a bowl. Pour in the melted mixture and stir to a stiff dough. Roll into walnut-sized balls (about 15 g each) and place on lined baking trays, spacing them at least 4 cm apart — they spread significantly.

Bake for 12–14 minutes until spread flat and darkened to a deep amber — they will crackle across the surface. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before moving to a wire rack; they break easily while hot. They should be fully rigid within 30 minutes of cooling. Store in an airtight tin.
